title: RONALD C. KESSLER The role of epidemiology in clinical ...
text:
[Music]
[Music]
I am an epidemiologist I'm not a
clinician until not too many years ago I
was not terribly interested in clinical
questions most of my work focus on
estimating the prevalence of mental
disorders risk factors for onset and
course of illness that could be used to
target interventions done by clinicians
but without me thinking a great deal
about what those interventions were how
effective they were but about a decade
ago I got involved in an epidemiological
study funded by dr. Insel here the in
the US Army in the United States
studying patterns of suicide among
people in the military and the explicit
goal of the study was not just to do a
descriptive analysis but to try to give
guidance to clinicians about what should
be done this is an enormous problem
although the suicide rate is going down
in most parts of the world it's
increasing in the United States
particularly among young people right
now there are about 20 suicides per day
in the United States among veterans
military veterans and the question is
what do we do about it well when we
start started doing the exempt
ideological studies we found somewhat to
my surprise that a substantial
percentage in many cases the majority of
the people who died by suicide had been
in treatment for mental health problems
the vast vast majority of course had a
history of psychopathology as discovered
by psychological autopsy studies after
the fact but the majority these people
were known to the treatment system in
most cases they were not in treatment at
the time of their death but they had
been in treatment and in often cases
were in treatment multiple times and
failed so the question is is is the
treatment of mental disorders that bad
that we that we can't prevent these
people from dying who are in our hands
and and as you know doubtlessly better
than I do I came to
after looking into the literature
treatments of mental disorders are are
in fact surprisingly good compared to
treatments of diabetes and asthma and so
forth but there is there are a couple
things that are distinct about
treatments of mental disorders that have
become the focus of my clinical
epidemiological studies over the
subsequent decade and I came to realize
that a epidemiological perspective is
something that's missing from clinical
research in a way that's very important
the problem is that there are many
effective treatments for common mental
disorders if we break an arm there's
only one thing we can do you go to the
ER you get your arm set but if you have
depression you can go to a minister or a
social worker or a licensed mental
health professional or a primary care
doctor who gives you a Danite depressant
medication or you can go to various
kinds of complicated psychotherapy Zoar
combinations or nowadays infusions
ketamine and so forth but none of those
things is optimal for all patients there
are a number of studies that show that
about if you take one of the
evidence-based treatments and randomly
assign it to a patient for depression
say about 30% well their episode will
remit and we know from the star D study
and other studies that have been done
subsequently if you take those people
who don't remit and take another when it
ran them and giveth them about 25% will
remit and if you take a third what it
ran them and give it to another maybe 15
to 20% will remit and if we can keep
people in treatment long enough it might
be that we get over 80 85 percent
remission the problem is that depressed
people are depressed and they give up on
treatment and we found in the military
that the typical pattern is they go into
treatment and they drop out and then six
months later they come in for another
kind of treatment and they drop out and
a year later they come into treatment
and
after two sessions dates they're not
doing well and they say the hell with it
they kill themselves
so we started thinking that really we
have this if we had kept those people in
treatment for the fourth and fifth and
sixth treatment in the vast majority of
cases we would have been able to do
something with them but we don't have
that many chances we have to figure out
how to get the right treatments to the
right patients right away and we're not
currently very good at that not much is
known about how to match the right
treatments to the right medication to
the right patients and as a result of
that in many areas of Medicine it's
psychiatry trial and error often
informed with some understanding of
pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics or
clinical comfort with a different with a
particular medication use over the years
governs treatment selection decisions
and that's especially true with instant
cases as opposed to people where this is
the fifth episode of schizophrenia you
know they responded to seroquel the last
time you give them circle again what do
you do with that first episode very
little is known the it's likely as I
said that the vast majority could be
helped we could stick with the treatment
through to the trial thing but it often
fails to happen and sometimes failure to
stick with treatment has tragic
consequences as a little interesting
aside I wasn't thinking of speaking
about this but I I just was telling
someone about this last night in the
world mental health service which I run
we asked people who have a history of a
particular disorder think of major
depression again how old were you when
it started your first episode have you
ever gotten treatment have you ever seen
a professional and it turns out that in
most developed countries the vast
majority have 60 70 percent of senior
professionals sometime in their life and
then we say to them have you ever seen a
professional who really helped you and
about half of them have
and then we ask another question for the
people who have seen a professional who
helped them we said how many different
professionals did you have to go to so
we found one who helped you and the
people who didn't get helped from the
professional they saw it well how many
did you try and it turns out that you
can estimate survival curves you can say
what's the conditional probability of
being helped the first time and for the
case of depression it's about 30 percent
if you're not helped the first time
what's the cumulative probability of
being helped the second time if you go a
second time and a third and fourth and a
fifth that it turns out that if we want
to get people up to the point where 90%
are helped in virtually all developed
countries in the world that we've
studied we studied 23 of them now you
have to go to five people five people
you have to go till two you get the
question is among the people who are not
helped the first time what percent go to
a second person of the people who are
not helped the second time what percent
persists and go to a third and a fourth
and a fifth and so forth it turns out
that the reason that there's the
discrepancy between the 50% who are
helped and the 90% who could be helped
because people quit people give up and
we started looking at predictors
epidemiological predictors of what are
the predictors of being helpful and
getting helpful treatment and we found
an interesting thing that there is a
significant association between time and
helpfulness of treatment people who
started their treatment after the
introduction of SSRIs are much more
likely to have been helped than people
who started treatment when they got a
TCA or Annamayya all else equal but
there is
not an association and that that's
that's that's the helpfulness of
treatment if you get it in other words
if you get the first treatment it's
significantly more likely to be helpful
if you got it after 1987 or so if you
get a second treatment it's
significantly more likely to be helpful
if you get it after 90 thousand than
before and so forth
however there's no association between
time and weather at the patient level
the patient says they were helped why is
that because in recent years younger
people are giving up quicker so the
older you are the less helpful treatment
was when you tried it but the more
likely you are to persist and as a
result the percent of people who were
helped nowadays is 50% the percent of
people who helped 20 years ago was 50%
and in our little Lancet Commission
Mario where we're talking about why is
there this white why is it that as
treatment has gone up prevalence of
depression has not gone up that's why
people try one time or two times and
they give up and the typical question is
have you ever gotten treatment it's not
whether you persist in entry whether you
went through the whole course going
through the whole course is critical now
one way of resolving that is to try to
make the treatment selection project a
process more evidence-based picking the
right treatment for the right person now
[Music]
precision medicine which is the for a
biological psychiatrist you know what
precision medicine is there's an
enormous amount of research that's been
carried out to find reliable
prescriptive predictors of differential
treatment response by the word
prescriptive in this literature we mean
a predictor that says that treatment a
is going to work better than treatment B
for this particular individual we have a
lot of information about that
psychiatrist to focus largely on
biomarkers but so far without a great
deal of success there's some interesting
stuff about neuroimaging and you know
there's these people doing things with
pharmacogenomic tests but they're not
really ready for primetime yet and
there's new stuff that's going to be
done with ketamine who are the people
who are most likely to respond to it and
so forth but not a great deal in terms
of clinical usefulness psychologists who
focus largely on clinical ratings and
patient self reports and performance
based neuro cognitive tests and actually
have had considerably better results but
psychological studies are typically very
small they're not funded by
pharmaceutical companies there's very
little replication and so forth the what
we need to do to move forward in this
line of work is four things and I'm
going to talk about those for today and
describe to you several projects I've
been involved in that are doing these
things I think this is the way forward
the first is we need comprehensive
predictor sets when we do research so
much of the work particularly the work
of biomarkers is interested in in one
particular hypothesis or one set of
biomarkers the the genomic kind of tests
or the fMRI tests or what have you but
looking at really a comprehensive set of
all the kinds of predictors that are
important and that means very long
baseline assessments second we need
comprehensive comparisons across the
full range of available treatments
typical studies in the precision
treatment world are looking at two
clinically you have many more than two
treatments we have to start locate a
network meta analysis doesn't help us
with this because we're looking at the
individual level for that particular
individual what's the right treatment
and so you can't do meta-analysis there
are other ways of doing meta synthesis
of individual level data but not will be
have right now and the third thing I'll
say more about this in a little while
because it has profound implications for
how we do these studies we need much
much bigger sample sizes the embark
study for example you know 300 to some
people 3 arms 2 phases there's 80 people
in it hopeless
it's just hopelessly underpowered and
that's the case with many of these
studies there's often the idea that this
is a preliminary study we're going to be
able to find things and then we can
replicate it in another study that
doesn't work because if you only have 80
people for arms you're not gonna be able
to see the signal and there's nothing to
replicate you're just going to be
replicating a bunch of noise we need
much much bigger samples and the
question is how do we have designs that
make that feasible and as you'll see at
a moment my sense is that clinical
trials are not the way to go and then
finally we need to have improved
statistical methods to develop optimal
precision step care systems and there's
a great deal of progress being made in
that area although there's a lot of
focus on can we do something there
that's really a problem that's pretty
much solved at this point it's the more
practical kind of low-level problems
that we have to deal with I'm gonna say
a little bit about each of these things
now comprehensive predictor says we
already know about quite a few
consistently significant predictors as I
mentioned and the but they've never been
included together in a single
prescriptive model so we finished an
analysis recently have looking at all
the replicated predictors of
differential response just too broadly
defined antidepressants alone
psychotherapy alone combined
very broad kind of thing they're about
35 predictors that have been replicated
as being significant predictors but
there's never been a study that's looked
at all 35 of them at the same time and
the best we have are studies that looked
at 15 of them at the same time with
about a sample that was about 20% as big
as it needed to be to be able to do
anything so we really don't have much of
anything the we need to know potentially
important predictors we need to be
considered and they include in
naturalistic studies which is where I'm
going to be going in a minute using
natural language processing of clinical
notes texts Facebook posts and so forth
to learn more than we do right now about
things they're important small area
geocode variables so for example in my
work on suicide one of the strongest
predictors of whether a veteran a
military veteran in the United States
will kill themselves is the unemployment
rate in their local neighbor market
which is not something we look at
typically and that's true even among
people who are hospitalized for a
suicide attempt here's a person who's
made a suicide attempt they're in a
hospital are they going to kill
themselves in the three months after
discharge one of the strongest
predictors is do they live in a place
where there's a high unemployment rate
something we never look at but it's
readily available and there are also a
lot of promising and scalable targeted
biomarkers that we can look at and I
should mention here although it's
something that's a little more complex
than we can talk about today that a lot
of what I call about scalable biomarkers
well the biomarkers are expensive to
collect either in terms of the costs of
the procedure itself or the time costs
of collecting the data and shipping the
data in process
so forth there are many cases where
there's no ranee to test the biomarker
so Lords we can with a series of
relatively inexpensive tests of
self-report measures right now say that
some very inexpensive EC BT PTSD coach
one of these things is sort of an odd
thing we can say with great assurance
that that's going to work well for that
patient we shouldn't be doing a 350
dollar pharmacogenomic test on that
person we already know an inexpensive
treatment is going to work and we also
know that there are certain
characteristics that say that the things
we have right now are not going to work
for that person we can narrow the people
who we give the expensive biomarker
tests to by tiered kinds of thoughtful
modeling procedures which is something
we don't do but again given the enormous
cost of doing the biological studies
that's something we have to do improve
statistical methods on the broad logic
of estimating prescription prediction
models is pretty clear it's things that
you all learned in the first statistics
course we have treatment a and treatment
B so we have an active comparator does
the person remit and we have a variable
like gender are they male or female and
we have an interaction between the two
things and if the interaction is
significant gender is a prescriptive
predictor so we know for example that
women do better than men with
interpersonal psychotherapy and men do
better than women with cognitive
behavior therapy men go better with
cognitive behavior therapy in my deep
clinical insights about this because men
don't really want to talk about it they
just want somebody to tell them the rule
and they will do it you know women on
the other hand do not want to rule they
want to talk about it so there's sort of
it's probably little little bit where
cops get at that but as I said I'm gonna
have to do me Alex just another question
but the idea is you find that when you
assign at random two kinds of
psychotherapy to people
men and women you have an interaction
the interaction is always significant in
that direction so if gender was the only
thing we know will be easy if those were
the only two treatments we have we would
give IPT to all women and cbt to all men
but it's not that simple because there
are other things there are aspects of
personality that interact just with
those two kinds of treatments there are
family history symptom profiles one of
the things I found that is again you
know is sort of a deep clinical insight
that I discovered in a number of studies
which is really and particularly in what
I'm involved in right now one of the
strongest predictors of medication
working psychotropic medication is
whether the patient takes the medication
and we can't know whether that's going
to be the case until after we try it
however there is a great deal of
interest in the literature on treatment
adherence and their scales that exists
that can be administered to people in
two or three minutes before treatment
starts that turn out to be very big
predictors and we were doing a study in
the in the Veterans Administration right
now where we've administered the scale
and we can just on the basis of that one
little two-minute scale say either that
this person should not be getting
antidepressants alone or they need some
kind of case management to hold their
hand to make sure they take it all by
itself just that one little two minute
thing
and in fact in typical routine care
that's not that's not used but it could
make an enormous difference I said
before that 30 percent of people do well
just at random in one treatment and
maybe 50% in - the difference between
just are you somebody who's going to
adhere or not adhere to your medication
it turns out that since there's 3 to 40%
and a 20% treatment response rate I mean
it's again it's not rocket science it's
just sort of kind of common sense and
it's it's surprising to me as an
outsider coming into the clinical world
there's a lot of deep thinking but
there's often a absence of
since so but the thing is when you do
these kind of models you typically find
10 or 15 different variables that have
significant interactions with treatment
a versus treatment B and it gets more
complicated when it's treatment a B and
C D the question is how do you put all
those together into one composite model
that says for this particular patient
who's a female with a certain IQ with a
certain personality with certain sir
that's up in history
okay what do I do for that person
weighing all those things together and
there are optimization techniques but
they're very seldom used the the best
algorithm the best way to come up with
those things has to do with the fact
that we often don't know that there are
the 15 significant predictors to begin
with the literature has said there's 30
that's works sometimes here and
sometimes never we don't know which one
is best we want to put all 30 in but
maybe only 15 will turn out to be
significant and we don't know which 15
so we have to worry about overfitting
and feature selection we have to worry
about the fact that typically the models
that are looked at assume some linear
association between some personality
characteristic and an additive
interaction it might not be that it
might be a very high order interaction
that some complicated neural networks
needed to capture and of course there
can be non additive ities as well so the
best way of dealing with this problem in
terms of statistics is to work with a
machine learning algorithm and a number
of you I'm sure are familiar with
machine learning algorithms that exist
for these purposes but as with with
therapies for treatment of mental
disorders there's no one machine
learning algorithm that's optimal for
all situations so you very often find
papers where people try two or three or
four different ways of doing it and
decide that this is the best one if you
go to R or Python which is you know
these these collection
computer programs there's currently
close to a hundred different machine
learning algorithms out there hundred
different ones and nobody tries all
hundred of them they tried three or four
that they're familiar with or something
that sounds interesting but there's
really not much in the way of systematic
ways of dealing with this but there are
as I mentioned a little while ago this
is now becoming pretty simple because
there's ensemble
machine learning methods and I won't go
into any detail about this other than
the mesh but there's something called
ensemble machine learning methods which
is that you could use a whole bunch
because computers are so big now we can
take a whole bunch of these different
algorithms run them in parallel carry
out simulations come up with weighted
averages and decide determine an optimal
way of combining all this information
there's also a way of doing this with
deep learning with multi level neural
networks although that has some problems
of overfitting so the statistical
methods part is not really a big issue
anymore we know how to do this although
in psychiatry it's sort of disappointing
that when you see papers doing machine
when are they still not doing optimal
things but the optimality there exists
the real problem is this simple thing of
sample size dimension precision
treatment models as I mentioned a minute
ago are at their heart and multivariate
models with a focus on pulling across
multiple significant interactions and
power to detect interactions as you know
statistical power is lower than to
detect main effects and that's
especially the case when we're looking
at composite predictors based on
multiple interactions especially when

detect meaningful nonlinearities above
marginal effects big big samples are
needed simulations have been done and we
found that precision treatment studies
in psychiatry in particular are just
hopelessly underpowered even for
something as simple as having treatment
a and treatment B and is the patient got
a remit from a major depressive episode
where you could expect 30 to 50%
remission rates
the minimum number of cases we need per

thousand patients they don't this star
Dee didn't even have that bigger thing
they had 4000 in total but they didn't

arms much larger samples needed for more
complex trials and less common outcomes
so some of the work I'm doing on suicide
for example I'll talk about in just a

ways now large trials of this sort exist
to evaluate common decisions in

how come well the the governing bodies
that decide how much money to put into
what disorder are largely made up of

they're pretty interested in

in depression all that much I think it's

needed to optimize treatment of mental

decisions to make the cardia trial was
really just where do you start the
statin is it at 180 total cholesterol
162 Ted you know I mean it's just a
simple little thing it's not anything
anywhere near as complex as what we have
in psychiatry so as a result of this I
think the only hope is the comparative
effectiveness research based on
observational
with embedded pragmatic trials it's much
more realistic now I don't know how many
of you familiar with this but there have
been there was a Cochrane review a

when you take well well carried out
comparative effectiveness studies
observational studies that risk adjust
for differences across patience and
observational data between fruit
endanger eaten be because they're not at
random and you adjust for loss to
follow-up using something called double
robust methods the results are
surprisingly similar to the results of
controlled clinical trials at a fraction
of the cost so the question is can we
use the same kind of design here and the
answer is yes there are four challenges
in doing that though one is we have to
get the measures of the prescriptive
predictors that I'm thinking now of of
clinical studies in observational data
sets that I'll jump forward to talk
about that in a minute the other is
balancing on baseline covariance that
people are not randomized to treatments
there's sometimes a certain people
always will get treated a certain way
and there's no way we can look at the
effect of differences in treatment for
those people it's got to be people or
close that close to equipoise the third
is we have to measure relevant outcomes
in the absence of measurement based care
which we don't have in psychiatry so how
do we know that treatment works and in
many cases as you know you don't the
patient seems to be doing well you
discharged her they're gone you have no
clue what's going on six months later
because mental disorders other than in
psychiatry and bipolar disorder are
treated incorrectly as if they're acute

conditions so we don't have a lot of
information about long term effects and
then we have to deal with loss to
follow-up because of that we don't have
those data now let me tell you about
three studies I'm involved in very
quickly that are addressing these

opioid death rate in the United
highest suicide rate east of the

in these very small rural communities
where health care is delivered in a

of these clinics are so small there's
not a physician there there's a nurse
there Monday through Friday and Monday
Wednesday and Friday morning a physician
comes or on Tuesday and Thursday
afternoon a physician comes that's how
simple they are they don't have mental

rates of depression fairly high rates of
anxiety disorder a lot of substances
order and it's all treated
pharmacologically by the primary care
doctor very little telepsychiatry
because it's such a poor state so we are
with the help of PIM and some others
looking to see what's the effect of
adding on an adjunctive internet-based
cognitive behavioral therapy to the
antidepressant treatment for these
people
it's a very large trial because of the
things I mentioned 6000 people we're
studying 6000 people in treatment for

getting a light version of Internet

management it's expensive by our stand
there's 15 million dollars but it's

it's something that at the end of the
day our hope is that we're going to be
able by having a rich baseline

patients shouldn't get this as an

later with

the the decision whether to hospitalized

hand it's tough to find a bed so if

there's all kinds of considerations that

were they hospitalized again did they

of those people whether 12 months are
dead or they've been rehospitalization
attempt sometimes they're dead from

among those people is 5 times higher
than the rest of the population so
probably they weren't all accidents but
the idea is that that's a very high rate
15% what happens when they're
hospitalized
fifteen percent makes absolutely no
difference to the outcome whether
they're hospitalized or not now does
that mean that hospitalization doesn't

that if that person wasn't hospitalized
you know he'll be dead two-hour so we

the dialectical behavioral therapy
people for example are very opposed to

hospitalized at a person in the wake of

and the thing is that's why the suicide

after discharged from the hospital
the suicide skyrockets they were just

that would be doing an enormous amount
of good remember there's 20 suicides a

that's a big success so we're doing the

the country of Taiwan or the renegade
